guide_description: Five days covering Barcelona's essentials plus Gaudí's hilltop park, the village atmosphere of Gràcia, and the Mediterranean waterfront. Each day is geographically self-contained.

days.cluster_tagline: Medieval Barcelona on foot - Cathedral, Roman walls, and the merchants' quarter
days.neighborhood_description: The two adjacent medieval quarters that form Barcelona's historic core. The Barri Gòtic is built on Roman foundations and centered on the Gothic Cathedral; El Born to the east is the former merchants' quarter, narrower, livelier, with the basilica of Santa Maria del Mar at its heart.

morning.notes: The 14th-century Gothic cathedral, often confused with Gaudí's Sagrada Família but architecturally entirely distinct. The cloister with its thirteen white geese (one for each year of Saint Eulalia's life, by tradition) is the unexpected centerpiece. The roof terraces are accessible by elevator for a small fee and give a rooftop view across the Gothic quarter that the Sagrada Família crowds rarely think to seek out.

afternoon.activity_title: Plaça del Rei and the Roman walls
afternoon.notes: The medieval royal square, framed by the Palau Reial Major where Columbus reportedly presented his findings to Ferdinand and Isabella in 1493. The MUHBA Barcelona History Museum entry on the square descends underground to a remarkably preserved 4 km of Roman Barcino - streets, dye works, wineries - directly beneath your feet. The most surprising museum visit in Barcelona.

evening.notes: Built 1329-1383 by the merchants and porters of the Ribera neighborhood, financed entirely by the working class - the local nickname is 'the Cathedral of the Sea'. The pure Catalan Gothic interior is unusually wide and airy compared to French Gothic peers, with proportions that have influenced architects from Gaudí onward. Late afternoon sun through the rose window is the visual reward.

if_time_permits.notes: The largest collection of Picasso's early work, housed across five connected medieval mansions in El Born. The museum's strength is showing Picasso before he was Picasso - the academic Barcelona years, the Blue Period, the 1957 Las Meninas variations. Closed Mondays. Free entry on Thursday afternoons and the first Sunday of each month - those slots queue heavily.

days.cluster_tagline: Modernisme Barcelona - the Sagrada Família, Casa Batlló, and the grid that planned them
days.neighborhood_description: The 19th-century planned extension of Barcelona beyond the old walls, laid out in 1859 on a uniform grid with chamfered corners that gives the neighborhood its octagonal-block visual rhythm. The grid hosts the densest concentration of Catalan modernisme (the Spanish art nouveau movement led by Gaudí, Domènech, and Puig) in the world.

morning.notes: Gaudí's unfinished basilica, begun 1882 and now expected to complete the central Jesus Christ tower in 2026. Book the timed-entry ticket online weeks ahead - walk-up is rare even off-season. Pick a morning slot for east-facing Nativity facade light through the stained glass, which floods the interior columns in blue and orange. The tower elevator tickets (one of the Nativity or Passion towers) are an additional fee and worth it for the close view of the spire detail.

afternoon.activity_title: Casa Batlló and Passeig de Gràcia
afternoon.notes: Gaudí's 1904 remodel of an existing apartment building - the curving bone-like balconies, the dragon-scale tiled roof, the chimney columns are all his. The interior tour is excellent (book online) but pricey; if budget-conscious, the facade view from across Passeig de Gràcia is itself a major attraction. Within the same block stands Casa Amatller by Puig i Cadafalch - the contrast between the two architects' styles is the point of the Manzana de la Discordia (Block of Discord).

evening.notes: Gaudí's last secular work, 1912 - locals nicknamed it 'La Pedrera' (The Quarry) for its raw stone facade. The rooftop, with its surreal warrior-helmet chimneys, is the famous part - timed-entry sunset tickets are worth the premium. The evening 'Pedrera de Nit' visit projects light shows on the chimneys and is more atmospheric than the daytime tour, though more expensive.

if_time_permits.notes: Domènech i Montaner's 1902-1930 hospital complex - 27 separate pavilions linked by tile-decorated walking paths and underground tunnels, designed on the radical theory that beauty itself accelerates healing. Recently restored and reopened to the public. Architecturally arguably more sustained than anything Gaudí built, but far less visited. UNESCO World Heritage in its own right.

days.cluster_tagline: The museum hill - Catalan art, the 1929 World's Fair architecture, and Mediterranean panoramas
days.neighborhood_description: The 184-meter hill rising from the western waterfront, redeveloped for the 1929 International Exposition and again for the 1992 Olympics. Holds the National Art Museum of Catalonia, the Joan Miró Foundation, the Olympic stadium, and the medieval castle at the top - plus the densest concentration of free panoramic views in Barcelona.

morning.activity_title: MNAC - National Art Museum of Catalonia
morning.notes: Housed in the 1929 Palau Nacional, the National Art Museum holds the world's most important collection of Romanesque frescoes - rescued from Pyrenean village churches in the 1920s and reinstalled here on reconstructed apse forms. The Romanesque galleries alone justify the visit; the modernisme wing upstairs covers Gaudí-era decorative arts. Free first Saturday afternoon and every Sunday after 3pm. The terrace gives a panoramic view back across the city.

afternoon.notes: Designed by Miró's friend Josep Lluís Sert (architect, MIT dean, exile from Franco's Spain), the foundation building is itself one of the great pieces of 20th-century museum architecture - white Mediterranean modernism with skylit galleries and a rooftop sculpture terrace. The collection is overwhelmingly Miró, organized chronologically, with a striking 'Tribute to Miró' room of works by other artists given in his honor.

evening.activity_title: Montjuïc Castle and the Mediterranean panorama
evening.notes: 17th-century military fortress at the summit, with a brutal political history - used by Franco's regime to imprison and execute Catalan nationalists. Now city-owned and open to the public. The view from the eastern ramparts covers the entire Mediterranean coast from the city out to sea; western views look down on the port and the Olympic ring. Easiest access from the cable car running up from the Telefèric station above Poble Sec.

if_time_permits.notes: Mies van der Rohe's German pavilion for the 1929 Exposition - one of the most influential buildings of the 20th century, the foundational statement of modernist architecture. The original was demolished after the fair; this is a 1986 reconstruction on the original site using the original materials and drawings. Small, free-to-circle exterior, modest entry fee for inside.

days.cluster_tagline: Hilltop Gaudí and Barcelona's village neighborhood
days.neighborhood_description: The northern slopes above the city, where Gaudí built his most photographed park, and the adjacent neighborhood of Gràcia - an independent town until 1897, still preserving small squares, low-rise buildings, and the most localized atmosphere in central Barcelona.

morning.notes: Begun in 1900 as a failed luxury housing development for Gaudí's patron Eusebi Güell - only two houses were ever built, and the city eventually bought the land as a public park in 1922. The famous Monumental Zone (the dragon staircase, the columned hall, the wave-tile terrace) now requires a timed-entry ticket; the surrounding park is free and worth wandering. Book the timed ticket online; walk-up is impossible. Get there at opening to beat tour groups.

walk_to_next.to: Plaça de la Vila de Gràcia

afternoon.activity_title: Plaça de la Vila de Gràcia
afternoon.notes: The central square of the former town of Gràcia, dominated by the 1862 clock tower (Torre del Rellotge), which served as the political beacon of the Catalan republican movement in the late 19th century. The square anchors the neighborhood - five minutes' walk in any direction puts you on tree-lined narrow streets, small bookshops, vermut bars, and tile-fronted bakeries. Best on a weekday afternoon when locals dominate.

evening.notes: Gràcia's living-room square, named after a 19th-century working-class cooperative. Less tourist-pressured than Plaça Reial in the Gothic Quarter and the better local spot to sit with a drink as the neighborhood empties out for the evening. Surrounding streets like Carrer de Verdi and Carrer de Torrent de l'Olla house some of Barcelona's best small restaurants. Mid-August every year, Gràcia's residents decorate their streets for the Festa Major, transforming the neighborhood for ten days.

if_time_permits.notes: Gaudí's first commissioned major work (1883-1888), built for a tile manufacturer - hence the densely tiled facade. Only opened to the public in 2017 after extensive restoration. Smaller and less visited than the other Gaudí UNESCO sites; an excellent counterpoint to Park Güell on the same day. Five-minute walk from Plaça de la Vila de Gràcia.

walk_from_main_route.from: Plaça de la Vila de Gràcia

days.cluster_tagline: Mediterranean Barcelona - the fishermen's quarter, the Olympic beach, and the harbor
days.neighborhood_description: The triangular peninsula between the old harbor and the Mediterranean, laid out in 1753 as a planned grid for sailors and dockworkers displaced when the Citadel was built. Now the most concentrated stretch of beach in central Barcelona, lined with seafood restaurants and the Olympic-era waterfront redevelopment.

morning.notes: Housed in the medieval Royal Shipyards (Drassanes Reials), one of the largest surviving Gothic civil buildings in Europe - massive vaulted stone halls built to construct Mediterranean warships in the 13th century. The collection centers on a full-scale replica of Don Juan of Austria's flagship from the 1571 Battle of Lepanto. The building itself is the bigger attraction.

afternoon.notes: Walk the harbor from the foot of La Rambla east along the Passeig de Joan de Borbó - the Barceloneta neighborhood proper is the narrow grid of tall, slim apartment blocks built on what was originally military reclamation land. The beach itself was created from scratch for the 1992 Olympics. The Frank Gehry Fish sculpture marking the eastern end of the beach is the landmark dividing Barceloneta from the Vila Olímpica.

evening.activity_title: Parc de la Ciutadella at sunset
evening.notes: Built on the site of a 1715 Bourbon fortress that was eventually torn down and turned into Barcelona's first public park in the 1880s. The 1888 Universal Exposition fairgrounds, the Catalan Parliament building, the zoo, and the cascade fountain (which the young Gaudí helped design as a student assistant) all live within the perimeter. A natural counterweight to the beach: green, quiet, locally used.

if_time_permits.notes: Brick-and-terracotta triumphal arch built as the ceremonial entrance to the 1888 Universal Exposition - not military like its Paris namesake but commercial, with friezes representing arts and industry. The Passeig de Lluís Companys promenade leading from the arch to the Ciutadella park is a wide tree-lined walk, popular with skateboarders.
